title: Financial Aid
summary: Financial aid is usually less about one big mystery and more about a sequence of forms, tasks, verification steps, and deadlines.

modes.label: Online
modes.summary: Start from the FAFSA guidance, complete the right form, and then keep checking MyASU for follow-up tasks.

steps.title: Start with ASU's FAFSA guidance
steps.description: Before you click through to the federal form, use the ASU page to understand which form or deadline applies to you.
bullets: Open the ASU financial aid guidance page.
Check whether you need FAFSA, CSS Profile, or another step for your situation.
Note any deadline or contributor information you will need.

visual.title: ASU guidance before the federal form
visual.caption: This keeps you from guessing which form or task matters most.

steps.title: Complete the form with the right documents nearby
steps.description: The process goes much faster if you gather tax, income, and contributor information before starting.
bullets: Go to StudentAid.gov from the ASU guidance page.
Use your and your contributor's FSA IDs if needed.
Submit the form and save the confirmation.
steps.reassurance: Almost everyone hits a confusing screen or missing-document moment here. That does not mean you are doing it wrong.

visual.title: Forms, IDs, and confirmation screens
visual.caption: Most of the friction is paperwork, not intelligence.

steps.title: Treat MyASU like the follow-up center
steps.description: Once the form is submitted, MyASU becomes the place where missing items, verification, and updates show up.
bullets: Check your MyASU tasks regularly after filing.
Open any hold, task, or verification notice right away.
Complete requested documents before waiting for the next update.

visual.title: Tasks, holds, and award follow-up
visual.caption: A lot of students think filing the FAFSA is the end. It is often the start of the follow-up phase.

modes.label: In person
modes.summary: Use the online guidance first, then bring the confusing task or form to an ASU financial aid contact point.

steps.title: Figure out the exact task or hold you need explained
steps.description: In-person help works best when you can point to the specific MyASU task, verification request, or missing document.
bullets: Open MyASU and take a screenshot or write down the task name.
Bring your ASU ID and any document request you received.
Know whether your question is about filing, verification, awards, or missing requirements.

visual.title: Bring the exact confusing screen with you
visual.caption: The clearer the task name is, the easier it is for staff to help quickly.

steps.title: Ask them to translate the task into plain language
steps.description: The best in-person help often starts with, “Can you tell me what this actually means and what I need to do next?”
bullets: Show them the task, hold, or document request.
Ask what the task is asking for in plain language.
Ask whether there is a deadline or a specific upload process.
steps.reassurance: A lot of first-gen students are not confused by the money itself so much as the system language. That is normal.

visual.title: Task translation and next steps
visual.caption: You are not asking them to “do it for you.” You are asking them to make the system readable.

steps.title: Finish the next task the same day if you can
steps.description: If the answer is simple and you have the document, try to complete the next step right away instead of putting it off.
bullets: Write down exactly what file or information is still missing.
Ask where to upload it or who to send it to.
Set a reminder to recheck MyASU after you submit it.

visual.title: One less task before you leave
visual.caption: Financial aid gets easier when you turn vague tasks into concrete actions.

modes.label: Drop-in
modes.summary: Use quick help or same-day support when you have a specific financial aid task, hold, or document question.

steps.title: Bring one focused question
steps.description: Drop-in help works best if you know the specific task, deadline, or hold you need explained.
bullets: Open MyASU before you go so you can show the exact message.
Write down the one thing you need answered first.
Bring any document request or financial aid email with you.

visual.title: Best for one concrete problem
visual.caption: Drop-in is usually strongest when you are trying to decode one specific task.

steps.title: Ask what the next action is, not just what the task means
steps.description: Do not leave with only an explanation. Leave knowing what to do, where to do it, and by when.
bullets: Ask exactly what document or information is needed.
Ask where it should be submitted.
Ask how long it usually takes to update after submission.
steps.reassurance: Financial aid language can feel intentionally confusing. It often sounds that way even to students who have done this before.

visual.title: What do I do next, exactly?
visual.caption: That one sentence usually gets you farther than asking for a general explanation.

steps.title: Record the deadline before you walk away
steps.description: Deadlines matter here more than perfect understanding, so save the due date and task name before you leave.
bullets: Put the deadline in your calendar immediately.
Keep the task name written down in case you need help again.
Check MyASU again after you complete the requested step.

visual.title: Do not rely on memory for money deadlines
visual.caption: A saved deadline turns a stressful unknown into a manageable task.

questionSet.defaultOpener: Hi, I am trying to make sure I understand my financial aid steps and I want to know what I should do next.
questionSet.defaultProTip: If you can, have MyASU open during the conversation. The task name or hold message matters more than a general description.
questionSet.firstTimeIntro: Financial aid systems are full of terms students are expected to already know. You are not behind for needing translation.

scenarioPlaceholders.class: I am worried school is getting harder because I am also stressed about paying for everything.
scenarioPlaceholders.money: I filed or started financial aid, but I still do not understand what tasks or documents I need to finish.
scenarioPlaceholders.overwhelmed: MyASU has tasks and I do not know which financial aid step matters first.
scenarioPlaceholders.schedule: I need to know what aid or deadlines might affect next semester planning.
scenarioPlaceholders.something-else: I know I need financial aid help, but I do not know how to explain the issue clearly.

first-year: Can you explain what this task or hold means in plain language?
What do I need to complete first so my aid stays on track?
Is there anything first-year students usually miss in this process?
How will I know in MyASU when the task is fully complete?
What deadline should I put in my calendar today?
second-year: What is the most important next financial aid step for me right now?
Do any of these tasks affect registration or billing timing?
Is there anything I should double-check before the next term starts?
What documents or follow-up requests are most common after this step?
What should I do if MyASU does not update after I submit something?
third-year: Are there aid or verification steps that could affect my next semester timeline?
What should I prioritize first if I have multiple financial aid tasks showing?
Is there anything that tends to delay aid at this stage?
How do I make sure I do not miss a requirement that affects disbursement?
What is the one question students usually do not think to ask here?
fourth-year-plus: Are there any financial aid tasks or deadlines that could affect finishing my degree on time?
What should I verify now before the next billing or aid cycle?
If my situation changed, what should I update first?
How do I know whether I still have unresolved aid items on my account?
What next step matters most from here?
